title: AI Trust & Safety Digest
tagline: A curated current snapshot of AI trust, safety, and governance, including as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
lead: Today’s strongest trust-and-safety signal is a continued shift from broad capability claims toward more targeted evaluation and governance scaffolding. The most relevant updates focus on agent evaluation quality, machine identity governance, interpretability and unlearning methods, and a new safety talent pipeline rather than major deployment or policy changes.

items.title: Claw-Eval proposes trajectory-aware safety evaluation for autonomous agents
items.why_it_matters: It targets a core gap in agent safety evaluation by scoring process, robustness, and unsafe behavior.

items.title: OpenAI launches pilot Safety Fellowship for independent alignment research
items.why_it_matters: It could expand the safety research pipeline and support more external alignment and governance work.

items.title: Machine Identity Governance Taxonomy maps AI system identities across enterprise and geopolitical boundaries
items.why_it_matters: It frames a growing governance problem around AI agents, tokens, service accounts, and cross-border control.

items.title: Exclusive Unlearning explores targeted removal of harmful knowledge from language models
items.why_it_matters: Better unlearning methods could improve post-training risk reduction without broadly degrading model utility.

items.title: LAG-XAI introduces a geometric framework for interpretable paraphrasing in transformer latent spaces
items.why_it_matters: Interpretability methods that expose latent structure can improve auditing and understanding of model behavior.

items.title: ACE-Bench offers configurable agent evaluation with controllable difficulty and longer horizons
items.why_it_matters: More reliable agent benchmarks can improve safety testing and reduce misleading aggregate performance scores.

items.title: Study examines how query rewriting affects bias in retrieval-augmented generation
items.why_it_matters: It highlights how standard RAG pipeline choices can mask or amplify retrieval biases.

items.title: Research argues retriever bias toward LLM-written text is driven by data, not model architecture
items.why_it_matters: This points to dataset interventions as a practical route to fairer, more reliable retrieval systems.

note: Selected from recent trust-and-safety relevant posts and papers by labs, evaluators, standards bodies, policy organizations, and arXiv, then summarized with GPT-5.4. Includes as many materially important fresh items as the day warrants.
